title_en: The Seerah — Early Life of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ
title_ar: السيرة النبوية — حياة النبي ﷺ في مكة
content_en:
Ibn Kathir's account of the Prophet's ﷺ early life in al-Bidayah wan-Nihayah draws on the most reliable biographical sources — Ibn Ishaq's Seerah (as transmitted through Ibn Hisham), the narrations in the six canonical hadith collections, and the biographical traditions preserved by Ibn Sa'd, al-Waqidi, and others — while applying his characteristic hadith-critical methodology to distinguish stronger from weaker narrations.

The birth of Muhammad ﷺ in the Year of the Elephant (approximately 570 CE) is described with attention to the signs that accompanied it. The Prophet's mother Aminah reportedly experienced an easy delivery and described seeing a light at his birth that illuminated the palaces of Syria — a narration recorded in authenticated hadith collections. The year of his birth coincided with the miraculous destruction of Abraha's army, which Ibn Kathir understands as a providential sign of divine protection over Mecca and the Kaaba in preparation for the prophet who would be born there.

The orphanhood of the Prophet ﷺ — his father died before his birth, his mother when he was six, and his grandfather Abd al-Muttalib when he was eight — is noted by Ibn Kathir as a deliberate divine arrangement rather than mere biographical misfortune. The Quran's reference to this: 'Did He not find you an orphan and give you refuge?' (Quran 93:6) — places the Prophet's orphanhood within the framework of divine care and mercy. The suffering of orphanhood that the Prophet ﷺ personally experienced may have been among the foundations of his extraordinary compassion for orphans and the vulnerable throughout his prophetic career.

Ibn Kathir gives extended treatment to the period of khatm al-nubuwwah — the completion of prophethood — including the famous story of the monk Bahira who recognized signs of prophethood in the young Muhammad ﷺ during a trading journey to Syria and warned his uncle Abu Talib to protect him. He covers the Prophet's ﷺ participation in the Hilf al-Fudul (the Pact of the Virtuous) — a pre-Islamic chivalric alliance for the protection of the weak — which the Prophet ﷺ later said he would honor even in Islam, because it was based on principles of justice.

The years of spiritual preparation — the increasing periods of retreat in the Cave of Hira, the true visions, and the Prophet's ﷺ innate recoiling from the idol worship of his society — are presented by Ibn Kathir as the divine preparation of a chosen vessel. Unlike ordinary human beings, Muhammad ﷺ was protected by Allah from many of the moral failings of pre-Islamic Arab society — he never worshipped idols, never participated in licentiousness, and was preserved in a moral integrity that made the sudden weight of prophetic revelation something his character was already prepared, in its essential orientation, to receive. The prophetic biography, in Ibn Kathir's account, is not a story of an ordinary person who happened to be chosen, but of a person whose entire life — including its apparent misfortunes — was shaped by divine providence for the mission he was destined to fulfill.
